schools.summary: The double title and shared faith: 'Simon Peter, a servant (doulos — bondservant) and an apostle of Jesus Christ, to them that have obtained (lachousin — received as a gift, by lot) like precious (isotimon — equally precious, of equal value) faith with us (the apostles) through the righteousness of God and our Saviour Jesus Christ' — Peter naming himself both servant and apostle (humility joined to authority), the readers given the same costly faith as the apostles (faith a gift, equal in worth for all). On 'the righteousness of God and our Saviour Jesus Christ,' Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott apply the Granville Sharp rule: the single article governing 'God and Saviour' calls Christ 'our God and Saviour Jesus Christ,' a direct affirmation of his deity (the righteousness of Christ, who is God), parallel to v.11 and Titus 2:13; a minority (the KJV punctuation) takes 'God' as the Father and 'our Saviour' as a distinct person.

Cyril: the phrase 'God and our Saviour Jesus Christ' confesses the deity of Christ, who is our God and Saviour; the righteousness by which the precious faith is given is the righteousness of Christ himself, true God, the single article binding 'God' and 'Saviour' to the one person of the Son.

debates.question: Does 'the righteousness of God and our Saviour Jesus Christ' call Christ God (one person) or name God the Father and the Saviour distinctly (two)?

sides.position: One person — the Granville Sharp construction calls Christ 'our God and Saviour Jesus Christ,' affirming his deity
Two persons — 'God' is the Father, 'our Saviour Jesus Christ' distinct (the KJV punctuation)
holders: the minority reflected in the Authorized Version punctuation

themes: Simon Peter, a servant and an apostle of Jesus Christ
like precious faith with us through the righteousness of God and our Saviour Jesus Christ
the precious faith and the deity of Christ
the Granville Sharp construction

schools.summary: The apostolic greeting: 'grace and peace be multiplied (plethyntheie — increased, abounding) unto you through the knowledge (epignosis — full, experiential knowledge) of God, and of Jesus our Lord' — the customary prayer for grace (God's favour) and peace (the resulting tranquility), here that they be not merely given but multiplied; grace and peace multiplied through the growing knowledge of God and Christ (the channel through which they increase, knowledge of God the key theme of the epistle), this knowledge being not bare information but the deep, personal acquaintance with God and Christ that nourishes the soul — the more one knows God and Jesus, the more grace and peace abound (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Grace and peace be multiplied unto you through the knowledge of God, and of Jesus our Lord
the prayer for grace and peace multiplied through knowledge
knowledge the channel of increasing grace and peace
the deep acquaintance with God and Christ

schools.summary: The gift: 'according as his divine power (the power of God, or of Christ) hath given (dedoremenes — granted, bestowed) unto us all things that pertain unto life and godliness, through the knowledge of him that hath called us to glory and virtue (arete — excellence)' — God's own power having supplied everything needed for spiritual life and godly living (nothing necessary lacking, the provision complete: 'all things' for both 'life,' spiritual and eternal, and 'godliness,' piety and holy living, the believer fully furnished for all that salvation and holiness require); this provision coming through the knowledge of God (or Christ), who called us to share his glory and moral excellence, the divine call being to glory and virtue, and knowledge of the Caller the channel (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: his divine power hath given unto us all things that pertain unto life and godliness
through the knowledge of him that hath called us to glory and virtue
God's divine power has given all things for life and godliness
the believer fully furnished for holiness

schools.summary: Barnes (with Calvin): 'whereby are given unto us exceeding great and precious (megista kai timia) promises: that by these ye might be partakers of the divine nature (theias physeos), having escaped the corruption that is in the world through (in) lust' — the gospel promises, surpassingly great and valuable, being God's means of sanctification; to be 'partakers (sharers) of the divine nature' meaning to share in God's MORAL nature (his holiness, purity, and likeness) by the renewal of the image of God in the soul — not a sharing of the divine essence or a becoming God (which would be impossible and pantheistic), but a real participation in God's moral character and life through the new birth; the partaking joined with 'having escaped the corruption' (moral decay) in the world through lust, the new nature and the flight from corruption going together. The promises sanctify by drawing the soul to God and from the world.

Cyril (and the patristic tradition) reads 'partakers of the divine nature' more strongly as theosis — a real participation in the divine life, the believer truly made a sharer in God by grace, deified (yet without confusion of essence, not becoming God by nature but partaking of God by grace through the Spirit and the promises); the escape from the world's corruption being the negative side of this transforming participation in the divine.

debates.question: What does it mean to be 'partakers of the divine nature'?

sides.position: Participation in God's moral nature — his holiness and likeness, the renewal of his image in the soul (not the divine essence)
Theosis — a real participation in the divine life, deification by grace (yet without sharing the divine essence)

themes: exceeding great and precious promises: that by these ye might be partakers of the divine nature
having escaped the corruption that is in the world through lust
the great promises by which we become partakers of the divine nature
moral participation in God's holiness, or theosis

schools.summary: The exhortation to active growth: 'and beside this, giving all diligence (pareisenenkantes spouden — bringing in alongside all earnestness, sparing no effort), add to (epichoregesate — supply in, furnish abundantly with) your faith virtue (arete — moral excellence, energy, courage); and to virtue knowledge (gnosis — practical wisdom, discernment)' — building on God's provision (vv.3-4), the believer to put forth every effort to grow in grace (the Christian life requiring diligent cultivation); the chain of graces beginning with faith (the foundation/root), to which moral excellence and energy are added (the first fruit), and to virtue knowledge (the understanding to direct one's virtue rightly), the graces linked, each building on the last (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: giving all diligence, add to your faith virtue; and to virtue knowledge
the call to diligently add virtue and knowledge to faith
the chain of graces built on faith
diligent cultivation of grace

schools.summary: The continuing chain of graces: 'and to knowledge temperance (enkrateia — self-control, mastery of the appetites); and to temperance patience (hypomone — steadfast endurance); and to patience godliness (eusebeia — piety, reverence toward God)' — the ladder continuing: knowledge to be governed by self-control (restraining the passions and appetites), self-control sustained by patient endurance (bearing up under trial), and endurance crowned by godliness (true reverence and devotion toward God); the graces moving from self-mastery toward piety, each built on the last (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: to knowledge temperance; and to temperance patience; and to patience godliness
the further graces: temperance, patience, godliness
self-control sustained by endurance, crowned by godliness
the ladder of graces continuing

schools.summary: The final links of the chain: 'and to godliness brotherly kindness (philadelphia — love of the brethren); and to brotherly kindness charity (agape — love)' — the chain culminating in love: godliness issuing in love for fellow-believers (the warm affection believers have for one another as members of God's family, piety toward God issuing in love toward the brethren), and brotherly love widening into charity (agape, the highest and most comprehensive grace, love that embraces not only the brethren but all, the fulfilling of the law and the bond of perfectness) — love the crown, summit, and goal of the whole chain of graces (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: to godliness brotherly kindness; and to brotherly kindness charity
the crowning graces: brotherly kindness and charity
love the crown and goal of the whole chain
brotherly love widening into universal charity

schools.summary: The condition and effect: 'for if these things (the graces just named) be in you, and abound (hyparchonta kai pleonazonta — exist and increase, present in growing measure), they make (cause) you that ye shall neither be barren (argous — idle, inactive) nor unfruitful (akarpous — without fruit) in the knowledge (epignosis — full knowledge) of our Lord Jesus Christ' — the graces required not merely present but abounding (possessed and growing); the abounding graces keeping the believer from being idle or fruitless, producing an active, productive Christian life, and making him fruitful in the knowledge of Christ (growth in grace and growth in the knowledge of Christ going together, the knowing not barren but fruitful) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: if these things be in you, and abound, they make you that ye shall neither be barren nor unfruitful
in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ
the abounding graces make one fruitful in the knowledge of Christ
grace and knowledge growing together

schools.summary: The contrast with the fruitful: 'but he that lacketh these things (the graces) is blind, and cannot see afar off (myopazon — shortsighted, near-sighted), and hath forgotten that he was purged (cleansed) from his old sins' — the one without the graces spiritually blind, or at best shortsighted (unable to see the distant, eternal realities, his spiritual vision darkened or contracted to the present, seeing only what is near, the things of this world, not the eternal); and having forgotten his conversion-cleansing from former sins (losing sight of the great mercy of his purging, failing to live accordingly, ingratitude and forgetfulness marking the unfruitful) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: he that lacketh these things is blind, and cannot see afar off
hath forgotten that he was purged from his old sins
the spiritual blindness of those who lack the graces
forgetting the cleansing from old sins

schools.summary: Barnes and Robertson (with Calvin): 'wherefore the rather, brethren, give diligence to make your calling and election sure (bebaian — firm, confirmed): for if ye do these things, ye shall never fall (stumble, apostatize)' — the believer making his calling and election 'sure' not by altering God's eternal decree (which is unchangeable) but by confirming it to himself, giving evidence through the diligent practice of the graces that he is truly called and elect (the good works the proof and assurance of election, not its cause); the election sure in God, diligence making it sure/certain/evident to the believer's own conscience; and diligent practice of the graces preserving from falling (the one who grows in grace kept secure, perseverance the fruit and evidence of true election).

Wesley and Clarke stress the earnest effort and perseverance: the believer is to be diligent, working out his salvation, the exhortation pressing the necessity of active obedience to confirm one's standing; 'if ye do these things, ye shall never fall' makes perseverance conditional on diligent practice of the graces — the warning and promise together urging the believer not to presume but to persevere.

themes: give diligence to make your calling and election sure
if ye do these things, ye shall never fall
making one's calling and election sure by diligence
the graces the evidence and assurance of election

schools.summary: The promise that crowns diligence: 'for so an entrance (eisodos — entry, admission) shall be ministered (epichoregethesetai — richly supplied, furnished) unto you abundantly (plousios — richly, with full measure) into the everlasting (eternal) kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ' — to the diligent, fruitful believer a rich, abundant entrance into Christ's eternal kingdom granted (not a bare admission but a triumphant, abundant entry, supplied richly as a victor welcomed in triumph, entering heaven not barely but gloriously with full assurance and welcome); the title 'our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ' (echoing v.1) again ascribing lordship and saving glory to Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: an entrance shall be ministered unto you abundantly into the everlasting kingdom of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ
the abundant entrance into the everlasting kingdom
a triumphant, abundant entry for the diligent
Christ's eternal kingdom

schools.summary: Peter's pastoral resolve: 'wherefore I will not be negligent (I will always be ready, not neglect) to put you always in remembrance (to remind you continually) of these things, though ye know them, and be established (firmly settled, grounded) in the present truth (the truth now possessed, the gospel)' — Peter purposing to keep reminding them of these truths (the duty of repeated reminder, even of known truths); the readers already knowing and grounded in the truth, yet reminder still needful (for even the established need to be stirred up and confirmed, the most settled believers benefiting from being reminded of known truths, memory being weak and the heart prone to forget, faithful repetition guarding against decline) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: I will not be negligent to put you always in remembrance of these things
though ye know them, and be established in the present truth
Peter's resolve to keep reminding them
even the established need reminder

schools.summary: The apostle's sense of duty: 'yea, I think it meet (right, fitting), as long as I am in this tabernacle (skenoma — tent, the body as the soul's temporary, movable dwelling), to stir you up (arouse, awaken) by putting you in remembrance (by way of reminder)' — Peter considering it his duty, while he yet lives in the body, to keep arousing them by reminder; 'tabernacle (tent)' picturing the body as a temporary dwelling for the soul (fittingly used by one soon to 'put it off,' v.14, the figure of life's brevity); 'stir you up' being to awaken and energize their minds lest they grow drowsy or forgetful (reminder not idle repetition but a rousing of the soul to alertness and diligence) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: as long as I am in this tabernacle, to stir you up by putting you in remembrance
Peter's duty to stir them up while he lives
the body as a temporary tent
reminder a rousing of the soul

schools.summary: Peter's certainty of his nearing death: 'knowing that shortly (soon, swiftly) I must put off (lay aside, as one removes a tent or garment) this my tabernacle (the body), even as our Lord Jesus Christ hath shewed (made clear, signified) me' — Peter knowing his death is near, to die being to 'put off' the tent of the body (the calm certainty of one facing the end, the figure softening death into a mere change of abode for the believer, the soul laying aside its dwelling); his foreknowledge resting on Christ's own word, who had foretold Peter's death (John 21:18-19) and perhaps recently signified its nearness (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: shortly I must put off this my tabernacle
even as our Lord Jesus Christ hath shewed me
Peter's awareness of his approaching death, as Christ foretold
death a putting off of the tent of the body

schools.summary: Peter's intention: 'moreover I will endeavour (spoudaso — be diligent, make every effort) that ye may be able after my decease (exodos — departure, death, a going-out) to have these things always in remembrance (to call them to mind continually)' — Peter purposing to provide a means by which, even after he is gone, they may continually remember these truths; 'decease (exodos — departure, exodus)' a gentle term for death (a going-out, the soul's departure not its end, the same word used of Christ's death at the Transfiguration, Luke 9:31); many taking the lasting remembrance as a reference to a written record (this epistle, or the Gospel of Mark, traditionally Peter's testimony), his concern being that the truth outlive him and be continually available (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: I will endeavour that ye may be able after my decease to have these things always in remembrance
Peter's care to provide a lasting remembrance after his death
decease a gentle term for the soul's departure
the truth secured beyond his lifetime

schools.summary: The contrast: 'for we have not followed cunningly devised (sesophismenois — cleverly invented, sophisticated) fables (mythois — myths), when we made known unto you the power and coming (parousia) of our Lord Jesus Christ, but were eyewitnesses (epoptai — spectators, those who saw with their own eyes) of his majesty (megaleiotetos)' — the apostolic message about Christ's power and (second) coming no cleverly invented myth (unlike the fables of false teachers and pagan religion); 'the power and coming' referring to Christ's majesty and his coming (his first advent in glory, or especially his second coming in power), the great themes grounded in fact not fancy; the apostles having personally witnessed Christ's majesty, especially at the Transfiguration (vv.17-18), the gospel resting on eyewitness testimony not invented tales (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the apostles were eyewitnesses (epoptai) of Christ's majesty — they beheld his glory with their own eyes at the Transfiguration; the gospel of his power and coming rests not on myth but on the direct testimony of those who saw the Son's majesty revealed.

themes: we have not followed cunningly devised fables, when we made known unto you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ
but were eyewitnesses of his majesty
the apostles preached no fable but were eyewitnesses of Christ's majesty
the gospel grounded in eyewitness testimony

schools.summary: The event recalled: 'for he received from God the Father honour and glory, when there came such a voice to him from the excellent glory (the magnificent glory, the bright cloud, the Shekinah, representing the Father's majestic presence), This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased' — at the Transfiguration Christ receiving honour and glory from the Father (the Father publicly honouring the Son before the eyewitness apostles); a voice coming from the glorious cloud, the Father himself speaking, declaring Jesus his beloved Son, the object of his good pleasure (the divine attestation of Christ's sonship, heard by the apostles, the visible 'excellent glory' the token of God's presence) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Cyril: the Father's voice from the excellent glory, 'This is my beloved Son,' is the divine witness to Christ's eternal sonship; at the Transfiguration the Father honoured and glorified the Son before the chosen apostles, attesting his beloved Son in whom he is well pleased.

themes: he received from God the Father honour and glory
This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased
the honour and glory Christ received from the Father at the Transfiguration
the Father's attestation of the Son

schools.summary: The personal testimony: 'and this voice which came from heaven we heard, when we were with him in the holy mount' — Peter (with James and John) having personally heard the Father's voice from heaven at the Transfiguration, the apostle bearing firsthand witness; the emphasis on 'we heard' (not report but personal hearing, the apostles' testimony to Christ resting on what they themselves heard and saw, the strongest kind of evidence); 'the holy mount' the mount of Transfiguration, called 'holy' because of the divine manifestation there (sanctified by the presence and voice of God, the apostles standing where God revealed his Son) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Cyril.

themes: this voice which came from heaven we heard, when we were with him in the holy mount
the apostles' hearing the voice on the holy mount
firsthand testimony of what they heard
the mount sanctified by the divine manifestation

schools.summary: The comparison and the figure: 'we have also a more sure (bebaioteron — firmer, more confirmed) word of prophecy; whereunto ye do well that ye take heed, as unto a light (lamp) that shineth in a dark (auchmeros — murky, squalid) place, until the day dawn, and the day star (phosphoros — the morning star) arise in your hearts' — 'more sure' read either as the prophetic word being more sure even than the voice on the mount (the written prophecies confirmed by their fulfillment, an abiding witness) or, comparatively, as the prophetic word now made more confirmed by the Transfiguration the apostles witnessed (the OT prophecy and apostolic eyewitness mutually confirming Christ); the prophetic word a lamp shining in the darkness of this world, which believers do well to heed, giving light amid the gloom 'until the day dawn' (the full light of day, the fuller gospel revelation, or Christ's coming) 'and the day star arise in your hearts' (Christ the morning star rising within, the inward illumination completing what prophecy began) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: We have also a more sure word of prophecy; whereunto ye do well that ye take heed
as unto a light that shineth in a dark place, until the day dawn, and the day star arise in your hearts
the sure word of prophecy as a lamp in the dark
the morning star rising in the heart

schools.summary: Barnes and Robertson (with Wesley and Clarke): 'knowing this first, that no prophecy of the scripture is of any private interpretation (idias epilyseos)' means that no prophecy originated from the prophet's own mind or private impulse — it was not the prophet's own invention, opinion, or disclosure but came from a higher source, God (settled by v.21, the explanatory 'for': the prophets 'spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost'). Barnes: the truths 'were not originated by themselves... but were of higher origin' (epilysis as 'disclosure'/'origination,' not 'interpretation'); Robertson: 'comes out of private disclosure,' not 'private interpretation'; Clarke: 'not by the mere private impulse of his own mind'; Wesley: 'it is not any man's own word.' Barnes adds the text is confined to prophecy, states a fact not a duty, says nothing of the Church's authority, and cannot bar the people from free access to Scripture.

Calvin: the genuine sense is that 'Scripture came not from man, or through the suggestions of man' — Peter bids us believe the prophecies as the indubitable oracles of God, since they did not emanate from men's own private suggestions; the word is set in opposition to divine revelation. Calvin sharply rejects the Roman Catholic conclusion that 'no interpretation of a private man ought to be deemed authoritative,' by which the Papists claim for their councils the chief right of interpreting Scripture: 'the Papists are doubly foolish,' for Peter does not prohibit each one from interpreting, but shows that whatever men bring 'of their own' is profane (were the whole world unanimous, still what proceeds from them is private). Gill agrees the prophecy is not of human origin.

debates.question: What does 'no prophecy is of private interpretation' mean?

sides.position: Prophecy did not originate from the prophet's own private mind or impulse, but came from God (the Protestant reading, settled by v.21)
Scripture is not to be privately interpreted by individuals, but only by the authority of the Church (the Roman Catholic reading)
holders: the Roman Catholic interpreters (named and firmly rejected by Calvin and Barnes)

themes: no prophecy of the scripture is of any private interpretation
prophecy not originating from the prophet's own mind but from God
the Protestant/Catholic crux of private interpretation
prophecy the oracles of God, not human invention

schools.summary: The great inspiration text explaining v.20: 'for the prophecy came not in old time (anciently) by the will of man (not at man's initiative, choice, or invention): but holy men of God (godly men, consecrated to God) spake as they were moved (pheromenoi — borne along, carried) by the Holy Ghost' — prophecy not arising from the prophet's own will or decision (not a human production); the prophets the human agents/instruments through whom (not from whom) the message came; the Holy Spirit bearing the prophets along as wind drives a ship, the words they spoke given and impelled by the Spirit — the classic statement of the divine inspiration of Scripture: God the author, holy men the instruments, making the prophetic word the very oracle of God (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: holy men spoke as they were borne along by the Holy Ghost — the Spirit moving and carrying the prophets so that their words were God's own; prophecy is the utterance of the Spirit through consecrated men, not the product of human will, the ground of its divine authority.

themes: the prophecy came not in old time by the will of man
holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost
prophecy came not by man's will but by the Holy Spirit's moving
the divine inspiration of Scripture
